title: Listening - Part 4: Flying Hot-Air Balloons
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Sophie Grant, a hot-air balloon pilot. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My final guest today is Sophie Grant, a hot-air balloon pilot. Sophie, what drew you to balloons? || Sophie: A family friend gave me a balloon flight for my eighteenth birthday. I expected to enjoy the landscape, and I did, but something else caught my attention. [24]I was fascinated by the pilot’s skill in using wind at different heights, since a balloon cannot simply be steered left or right[/24]. I began helping the crew at weekends and later trained to fly. || Presenter: What is most challenging for trainee pilots? || Sophie: People imagine taking off is the difficult moment because it looks dramatic. Landing is the greater challenge. [25]You have to choose a safe landing area while understanding that changing wind may take you somewhere different from the place you hoped for[/25]. Training teaches you to keep alternatives in mind throughout a flight. || Presenter: Do passengers often feel anxious? || Sophie: Some do, especially when the burner first makes its loud noise. Talking helps, but I also involve them. [26]Before take-off, I give passengers simple instructions and explain what will happen, because knowing their role makes them feel much more secure[/26]. Most become relaxed once we are floating calmly. Afterwards, several tell me that the preparation was what stopped them worrying during take-off. || Presenter: You must sometimes cancel flights. || Sophie: More often than I’d like. A beautiful sunny day may still have winds that are too strong. [27]Passengers may be disappointed, but I never fly simply to avoid upsetting people when conditions are not suitable[/27]. Safety decisions are easier when you are clear about them from the beginning. || Presenter: Why do so many flights happen very early in the day? || Sophie: It isn’t because pilots enjoy waking up in darkness! [28]Soon after sunrise, the air is generally cooler and steadier, before the sun creates the stronger movement that makes ballooning less predictable[/28]. Evening flights are possible too, when conditions settle again. || Presenter: What experience stays in your memory? || Sophie: Once we landed beside a farm after a wonderful flight. We expected the farmer to be irritated. [29]Instead, the family came out to help us gather the balloon and said they loved seeing it above their fields[/29]. That unexpected welcome captured the friendly spirit surrounding ballooning. || Presenter: What would you like to change in future? || Sophie: Balloon flights use fuel for the burner and vehicles following on the ground. [30]I’m interested in projects testing quieter, more efficient equipment that could reduce our environmental impact[/30]. The beauty of floating over the countryside should not prevent us thinking responsibly about how we do it.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What particularly interested Sophie during her first balloon flight?

options.A: The technical way the pilot made use of the wind.
options.B: The chance to see familiar places from above.
options.C: The teamwork required to prepare the balloon.

questions.explanation: She was fascinated by how a pilot uses winds at varying heights without direct steering.

questions.question: What does Sophie say trainee pilots find most challenging?

options.B: Selecting a landing place despite changing conditions.
options.C: Operating the burner during cold weather.

questions.explanation: Landing requires choosing safe options while recognising that winds may alter the destination.

questions.question: How does Sophie help nervous passengers?

options.A: She lets them choose where to stand.
options.B: She keeps the flight as short as possible.
options.C: She explains procedures and gives them a role.

questions.explanation: Passengers feel safer after instructions and knowing what they should do.

questions.question: What does Sophie say about cancelling flights?

options.A: She will cancel even if passengers are unhappy.
options.B: She offers disappointed passengers another activity.
options.C: She only cancels when bad weather is visible.

questions.explanation: She refuses to fly in unsuitable conditions merely to prevent disappointment.

questions.question: Why are balloon flights commonly scheduled early in the morning?

options.A: There is less traffic near landing areas.
options.B: The air usually makes flying more controllable.

questions.explanation: Cooler, steadier early air makes ballooning less unpredictable.

questions.question: What made one landing especially memorable for Sophie?

options.A: The balloon was landed in a particularly difficult place.
options.B: The passengers invited local people to join them.
options.C: The landowners reacted in a helpful and welcoming way.

questions.explanation: A farming family helped collect the balloon and welcomed seeing it over their fields.

questions.question: What is Sophie interested in for the future?

options.B: Giving more young people their first flight.

questions.explanation: She wants quieter, more efficient technology that can reduce environmental impact.
